params.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup appears. Student's data visible. All four scale options (1, 2, 5, 10) showing checkmarks.

beats.text: In Warmup, all four scales worked for your data. You had options!

elements.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup. Student's data with all four scales showing checkmarks.

beats.text: But what happens when the numbers are bigger? Let's find out.

elements.description: Scale Preview System from Warmup. Student's data with all four scales showing checkmarks.

params.description: Data table appears. Books Read This Month. Aisha: 20, Ben: 35, Carlos: 55, Dana: 80.
Vertical bar graph appears. Books Read This Month. Axis 0-50. Scale of 5 pre-selected. Graph initially empty.

beats.text: Here's data about how many books four students read this month. Dana read the most—80 books!

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Books Read This Month. Axis 0-50. Scale of 5 selected. Graph empty.

beats.text: Let me try a scale of 5 and see what happens.

params.description: Preview generates. Aisha (20), Ben (35), Carlos (55) bars appear normally. Dana's bar (80) extends past the graph boundary. Warning indicator appears.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Aisha, Ben, Carlos bars visible. Dana's bar extends past top. Warning indicator showing.

beats.text: Hmm. Carlos's bar is just above my graph, but Dana's bar goes past the top. The graph only goes up to 50 right now.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Aisha, Ben, Carlos bars visible. Dana's bar extends past top. Warning indicator showing.

beats.text: I could make the graph taller to fit 80. Let's see... I'd need 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80...

params.description: Axis extends upward. Tick marks appear: 55, 60, 65, 70, 75, 80. Axis now crowded with many tick marks.
Graph axis now extends to 80. All bars visible. Dana's bar reaches 80.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-80 with 17 tick marks visible (0, 5, 10, 15... 75, 80). All bars visible. Dana's bar at 80.

beats.text: That's a lot of numbers on the axis. 0, 5, 10, 15, 20... all the way up to 80. That's 17 tick marks.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-80. All 17 tick marks highlighted. All bars visible.

beats.text: It works, but is there a better way? When one scale needs too many tick marks, try a bigger scale.

elements.description: Data table showing Books Read This Month values.
Vertical bar graph. Axis 0-80 with 17 tick marks. All bars visible.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Books Read data. Mystery=35, Fantasy=55, Science=20, History=75. Current scale is 5, showing 17 tick marks (0, 5, 10...85). Scale Preview System interactive, Scale of 5 currently selected.

beats.text: Scale of 5 works, but that's a lot of tick marks. Let's try a bigger scale.
Click 'Scale of 10' to see what happens.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 10 button

params.description: Graph updates to Scale of 10. Axis now shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80—only 9 tick marks. All four bars fit cleanly. Checkmark indicator appears.

beats.text: Right. Look at that, only 9 tick marks now instead of 17. Much cleaner!
All the data fits, and the graph is easier to read.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph with Scale of 10. Books Read data. Mystery=35, Fantasy=55, Science=20, History=75. Axis shows 9 tick marks (0, 10, 20...80). Checkmark indicator visible.
Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Books Read data. Scale Preview System interactive. Scale of 5 currently selected showing 17 tick marks. click_scale_button tool active.

params.description: Data table appears showing data values. Largest value (80) is highlighted.

beats.text: Here's the first thing to check: What's your biggest number? That tells you what scales might work.
What's the biggest number in this data?

validator.description: Student selected 80, correct

beats.text: Right. 80 is the biggest. Always check the biggest number first. Does it fit on the scale without too many lines on the axis?

validator.description: Student selected 20 instead of 80

params.description: 80 highlighted in the data table.

beats.text: 20 is in the data, but it's not the biggest. We need the largest value. Look at all the numbers. Which one is biggest?

validator.description: Student selected 35 instead of 80

params.description: 80 highlighted in the data table.

beats.text: 35 is in the data, but there's a bigger number. Look at all four values. Which one is the greatest?

validator.description: Student selected 55 instead of 80

params.description: 80 highlighted in the data table.

beats.text: 55 is close, but not the biggest. Compare all the numbers. Which value is larger than 55?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Values highlight in order: 20, 35, 55, 80. 80 remains highlighted as the largest.

beats.text: Let me show you. We compare all the values: 20, 35, 55, 80. The biggest number is 80. When choosing a scale, always check the biggest number first. That tells you what scales can fit all your data without going past the graph boundary.

elements.description: Data table showing data values. Largest value (80) highlighted.

beats.text: You saw that Scale of 5 needed 17 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 9. For big data, bigger scales make cleaner graphs.
When more than one scale fits, pick the one that's easier to read.

elements.description: Data table showing data values. Largest value (80) highlighted.

params.description: Data table appears. Marbles in Jars context. Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23.
Horizontal bar graph frame appears with scale selector showing all four scale buttons. No bars displayed yet.

beats.text: Here's data about marbles. Look at the biggest number: 23. That's pretty small!

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values.
Horizontal bar graph frame with scale selector. Four scale buttons visible. No bars displayed yet.

beats.text: This graph is horizontal. The bars go sideways. Let's check: which scales fit? Try clicking each scale.
Click each scale to see if 23 fits.

validator.description: Student explored all four scales. All fit.

beats.text: That's right. All four scales fit!

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values.
Horizontal bar graph with scale selector. All four scale options previewed, all showing fit indicator.

beats.text: Let's look at Scale of 1.
Click Scale of 1 to see what it looks like.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 1.

params.description: Bar graph displays with Scale of 1. All bars fit. Axis shows 24 tick marks (0 through 23).

beats.text: Nice. It works. Every number lands exactly. But look at all those lines on the side! Small data means all scales fit. But some are cleaner than others.

validator.description: Student clicked a scale other than 1 on first attempt

beats.text: That one works too. Now click Scale of 1.

validator.description: Student clicked a scale other than 1 on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 1 button highlights.

beats.text: We want to see what Scale of 1 looks like. It's the button on the left. Click it.

validator.description: Student clicked a scale other than 1 three or more times. System models the action.

params.description: System clicks Scale of 1 button. Graph updates to show Scale of 1.

beats.text: Let me click Scale of 1 for us. The button on the left selects Scale of 1. When I click it, the graph updates to show what the data looks like with that scale. The axis now shows tick marks at every whole number from 0 to 23. That's 24 tick marks total. Scale of 1 shows every value exactly, but it creates a lot of marks on the axis for this data.

elements.description: Data table showing Marbles in Jars values.
Horizontal bar graph. Scale of 1. Bars for Jar A: 7, Jar B: 12, Jar C: 19, Jar D: 23. Axis displays 24 tick marks (0-23).

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Marbles data. Jar A=7, Jar B=24, Jar C=19. Scale of 1 active. 24 tick marks visible on vertical axis.
Data table showing Marbles values alongside graph.

beats.text: Scale of 1 works, but that's a lot of lines. You learned bigger scales are usually easier to read.
Let's try Scale of 10.
Click 'Scale of 10' to compare.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 10

elements.description: Vertical bar graph. Scale of 1 active. Marbles data. 24 tick marks visible.
Data table showing Marbles values.

warnings.message: 7 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle
19 won't land exactly on a line or in the middle

params.description: Scale changes to 10. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30. Bars appear. Jar A (7) between 0 and 10. Jar C (19) between 10 and 20. ⚠️ indicators on Jar A and Jar C.

beats.text: Hmm. Scale of 10 fits, but there are only 3 marks on the axis. Look at those warnings. Can you tell the exact value of where those bars end? Someone looking at our graph without the data table would need to guess how many marbles are shown.

For big data, we'd pick Scale of 10 anyway. It's the easiest to read because we don't want too many lines on the axis. But these numbers are small and it's hard to find the exact location of the bar on the graph. We can do better.
When data is small, you have room to find a scale where everything lands exactly.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph. Scale of 10 active. Marbles data. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30. Jar A and Jar C have ⚠️ indicators showing values don't land exactly.
Data table showing Marbles values.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Marbles data. Red=7, Blue=12, Green=9, Yellow=5. Currently showing Scale of 10. Scale selector interactive.

beats.text: Let's try a smaller scale. Try a Scale of 2.
Click 'Scale of 2' to preview.

validator.description: Student clicked Scale of 2 button

params.description: Scale transitions to 2. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Green checkmark indicator appears. No warnings.

beats.text: Right. We can read the end of the bars clearly. All the numbers land exactly. 7 is right at 7, exactly halfway between 6 and 8. 12 is right at 12.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Scale of 2. Marbles data. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Green checkmark visible.

validator.description: Student clicked wrong scale button on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look for the Scale of 2 button and click it.

validator.description: Student clicked wrong scale button on second attempt

params.description: Scale of 2 button highlights.

beats.text: Here's what to try: click the button that says 'Scale of 2'. It's part of the scale selector.

validator.description: Student clicked wrong scale button three or more times. System models the correct action.

params.description: System demonstrates: Scale transitions to 2. All bars land exactly on tick marks. Green checkmark indicator appears.

beats.text: Let me show you. I click the Scale of 2 button. The scale changes and now all the bars land exactly on tick marks. With Scale of 2, half of 2 is 1, so every whole number lands right on a line or exactly halfway between two lines.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Marbles data showing Scale of 10. Scale selector active with Scale of 2 option available.

beats.text: With Scale of 2, half of 2 is 1. So you can show any whole number exactly.
Scale of 2 works to show the exact value for all numbers, and for small data, the graph won't have too many marks on the axis.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Scale of 2. Marbles data. All bars land exactly on tick marks.

params.description: Data Set A displayed on left side. Values: 20, 35, 55, 80. Label: 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Data Set B displayed on right side. Values: 7, 12, 19, 23. Label: 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

beats.text: Here's a quick way to know if you need Scale of 2. Check the last digit of each number in the ones place.
Numbers that end in 0 or 5, like 20, 35, 55, 80, those are multiples of 5 and can be shown clearly with Scales of 5 and 10.

But look at 7, 12, 19, 23. See the digits in the ones place? 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5. That tells you they aren't multiples of 5 so Scale of 2 might be your best choice. Check our groups. Which data set has last digits that are NOT 0 or 5?
Which data set has last digits that are NOT 0 or 5?

validator.description: Student selected Data Set B (7, 12, 19, 23), correct

beats.text: Right. Look at those last digits: 7, 2, 9, 3. None are 0 or 5. That's your Scale of 2 signal.

validator.description: Student selected Data Set A (20, 35, 55, 80)

params.description: Data Set A highlights. Ones digits (0, 5, 5, 0) emphasized.

beats.text: Look at the ones place in Data Set A. Every number ends in 0 or 5. Those are multiples of 5.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Data Set B ones digits (7, 2, 9, 3) highlight in sequence.

beats.text: Let me show you. Look at Data Set B: 7, 12, 19, 23. The ones place shows 7, 2, 9, 3. None of those are 0 or 5, so these aren't multiples of 5. That pattern tells you Scale of 2 is the right tool. Data Set B is the answer.

elements.description: Data Set A on left. Values: 20, 35, 55, 80. Label: 'Ones digits: 0 or 5'.
Data Set B on right. Values: 7, 12, 19, 23. Label: 'Ones digits: 7, 2, 9, 3'.

params.title: Points Scored
params.description: Vertical data table appears. Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: Here's data from a game. Four rounds of points: 22, 15, 8, 31.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: Check the ones place digits: 2, 5, 8, 1. Do they all end in 0 or 5?

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: 22... no. 15... yes. 8... no. 31... no. Three of them don't, so they aren't multiples of 5. Which scale will show all values exactly?
Which scale will show ALL values exactly?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2, correct

params.title: Points Scored
params.description: Vertical bar graph appears with scale of 2. Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31. All bars fit well, exact values clear.

beats.text: Right. Scale of 2. All of the bars fit well on the graph and their exact values are clear.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 1

params.description: Preview of Scale of 1 appears showing 32 tick marks from 0 to 31.

beats.text: Scale of 1 works for showing exact values. But count those marks: 32 lines to reach 31. That's a lot. Is there a cleaner way?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Values 22, 8, and 31 highlight in the data table.

beats.text: Look at 22, 8, 31. Their ones place digits are 2, 8, 1. None are 0 or 5, so they won't land exactly on Scale of 5. Which scale shows any whole number exactly?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 10

params.description: Values 22, 8, and 31 highlight in the data table.

beats.text: Scale of 10 is clean, but look at 22, 8, 31. None end in 0 or 5, so they won't land exactly on the tick marks. We need a scale that shows any whole number exactly.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Bar graph with Scale of 2 appears. All four bars displayed at correct heights.
Each bar pulses to show it lands exactly on a line or halfway between lines. 22 is at 22 (on the line), 15 is halfway between 14 and 16, 8 is at 8 (on the line), 31 is halfway between 30 and 32.

beats.text: The answer is Scale of 2. Here's why: when values don't end in 0 or 5, Scale of 2 shows them exactly. Half of 2 is 1, so every whole number lands either on a line or exactly halfway between. 22 is on the line at 22. 15 is exactly halfway between 14 and 16. 8 is on the line at 8. 31 is exactly halfway between 30 and 32. Scale of 2 works for any whole number.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

params.title: Points Scored
params.description: Second vertical bar graph appears. Scale of 1. Same Points Scored data. Side-by-side comparison with Scale of 2 graph. Same height bars, only axis markings differ.

beats.text: Scale of 1 also shows the exact values, but it would require a lot of marks on the axis to get to the greatest value of 31. Let's consider when we might choose a scale of 1.

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Points Scored data. Round 1: 22, Round 2: 15, Round 3: 8, Round 4: 31.

beats.text: You got it. Every number lands right on a line. No halfway bars needed. We can read the graph accurately without having the data table.
For very small numbers, Scale of 1 can be a great choice: simple and exact.

params.description: Vertical data table appears. Favorite Fruits data: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Horizontal bar graph placeholder with scale preview system available. All three scales (2, 5, 10) show checkmarks indicating they are valid options.

beats.text: Here's data about students' favorite fruits. The most votes went to Dates with 60. Scale of 1 doesn't make sense here because it would take 60 lines on the axis. That's too much!

elements.description: Vertical data table showing Favorite Fruits data: Dates 60, Cherries 45, Bananas 30, Apples 15.
Horizontal bar graph with scale preview system. All three scales (2, 5, 10) show checkmarks.

beats.text: Your job: pick the right scale. Which scale will you use?
Which scale will you use?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 10, correct

beats.text: Good choice. Scale of 10 works well here.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2

params.description: Dates value (60) highlighted in data table.

beats.text: Scale of 2 works, but look at the biggest number: 60. That means 30 tick marks on the axis. With Scale of 10, you only need 6 tick marks. Which is easier to read?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5

params.description: Scale of 5 and Scale of 10 options highlighted side by side.

beats.text: Scale of 5 fits all the data. But look: to reach 60, you need 12 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 6. Both work, but which graph is cleaner?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System demonstrates Scale of 10. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60. All bars fit cleanly: Dates at 60, Cherries at 45, Bananas at 30, Apples at 15.

beats.text: Here's how I think about it. The biggest number is 60. All values end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 5 and Scale of 10 both show exact values. But Scale of 10 only needs 6 tick marks while Scale of 5 needs 12. Fewer marks make the graph easier to read. The answer is Scale of 10 because it balances precision with clarity.

validator.description: Branch 4: Student selected mix of correct and incorrect answers

params.description: Correct selections highlighted in green.

beats.text: Some of your choices are right. But check the others carefully. Do they all explain why Scale of 10 is a good choice for this specific data?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights each reason in sequence: ones place digits showing 0 or 5, axis with only 6 clean tick marks, all bars fitting within range, comparison showing Scale of 2 would need 30 marks.

beats.text: Let me walk through why Scale of 10 works. First, all values end in 0 or 5, so they land exactly on tick marks or halfway between. Second, the graph is easy to read with only 6 marks on the axis. Third, the biggest number is 60, so all bars fit well. Fourth, Scale of 2 would need 30 marks to reach 60, which is too many. All four reasons explain why Scale of 10 is the best choice here.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2, correct

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

beats.text: Let me show you. Look at the ones place: 7, 4, 1, 3. None are 0 or 5, so these aren't multiples of 5. Scale of 2 works because half of 2 is 1. That means we can show any whole number exactly. The answer is Scale of 2.

With Scale of 2, half of 2 is 1, so every whole number lands exactly on a line or halfway between. The bars are: Guitar 27, Piano 14, Violin 41, Drums 33.

beats.text: You've done this vertical and horizontal now. Same process every time: check the numbers, choose the scale.
Which scale works best for this data?

validator.description: Student selected scale of 10, most efficient for this data

beats.text: Nice. All values end in 0 or 5, and the biggest is 70. Scale of 10 gives you a clean graph.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5 on first attempt

beats.text: Scale of 5 works for these numbers. But check if there's a bigger scale that's even easier to read.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2 on first attempt

beats.text: Scale of 2 fits. But 70 is a big number. Try a bigger scale to keep the axis cleaner.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 1 on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Count to 70 by ones. That's a lot of marks on the axis. Try a bigger scale.

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 5 on second attempt

params.description: Data values highlighted showing ones place digits: 5, 5, 0, 0.

beats.text: Look at the last digits: all 0 or 5. These are multiples of 5. Both scales of 5 and 10 work. Which gives fewer marks on the axis to reach 70?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 2 on second attempt

params.description: Data values highlighted showing ones place digits: 5, 5, 0, 0.

beats.text: Check the ones place: 5, 5, 0, 0. They're all 0 or 5, so these are multiples of 5. What's the biggest scale where all these numbers land exactly?

validator.description: Student selected Scale of 1 on second attempt

params.description: Largest value (70) highlighted.

beats.text: The biggest number is 70. Count by ones to 70: that's 70 tick marks. For bigger data, a bigger scale makes the graph easier to read. Which scale reaches 70 with the fewest marks?

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System highlights ones place digits: 5, 5, 0, 0. Then shows comparison: Scale of 5 needs 14 marks (0, 5, 10...70), Scale of 10 needs 8 marks (0, 10, 20...70).

beats.text: Let me show you. All values end in 0 or 5, so both scales of 5 and 10 work. But look: Scale of 5 needs 14 tick marks to reach 70. Scale of 10 only needs 8. Same data, cleaner graph. For big data that's multiples of 5, Scale of 10 is best.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.

params.description: Horizontal bar graph creation tool appears with scale of 10. Axis shows tick marks at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80. Empty bars ready for student to set heights.

beats.text: Now create the graph. Place each bar at the right length.
Click to place the bar for Red Team (25 points).

validator.description: Student set Red Team bar to 25

params.description: Red Team bar locks at 25.

beats.text: Right. 25, halfway between 20 and 30.

validator.description: Student set Red Team bar to 20 on first attempt

beats.text: Check the data table. How many points did Red Team score?

validator.description: Student set Red Team bar to 30 on first attempt

beats.text: Close. Red Team scored 25, not 30. Where does 25 land?

validator.description: Student set Red Team bar to any other wrong height on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Find 25 on the axis. It's halfway between two marks.

validator.description: Student set Red Team bar to 20 on second attempt

params.description: Red Team row highlighted showing value 25.

beats.text: Look at the data table. Red Team scored 25 points, not 20. Find the mark for 20, then go halfway to 30.

validator.description: Student set Red Team bar to 30 on second attempt

params.description: Axis marks at 20 and 30 highlighted.

beats.text: Red Team scored 25, not 30. Look at the axis marks: 20 and 30. Where does 25 go? Exactly halfway between those two marks.

validator.description: Student set Red Team bar to any other wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Axis marks at 20 and 30 highlighted with midpoint indicator.

beats.text: Red Team scored 25. The axis goes by tens: 0, 10, 20, 30. Find 20 and 30. 25 is exactly halfway between them. Place the bar there.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates: highlights axis marks at 20 and 30, shows midpoint indicator at 25, then draws bar to that height. Bar locks at 25.

beats.text: Watch. Red Team scored 25. I find 20 and 30 on the axis. 25 is halfway between them. With Scale of 10, any number ending in 5 lands exactly at the midpoint. The bar for Red Team goes to 25.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. Red Team bar set at 25. Other bars empty.

beats.text: Click to place the bar for Blue Team (40 points).

validator.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 40

params.description: Blue Team bar locks at 40.

beats.text: Right. On 40.

validator.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 30 on first attempt

beats.text: Check the data table. How many points did Blue Team score?

validator.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 50 on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Blue Team scored 40, not 50. Find 40 on the axis.

validator.description: Student set Blue Team bar to any other wrong height on first attempt

beats.text: Look at the data table. Blue Team scored 40. That's right on a mark.

validator.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 30 on second attempt

params.description: Blue Team row highlighted showing value 40.

beats.text: Check the data table. Blue Team scored 40, not 30. Find the mark labeled 40 on the axis.

validator.description: Student set Blue Team bar to 50 on second attempt

params.description: Axis mark at 40 highlighted.

beats.text: Blue Team scored 40, not 50. The number ends in 0, so it lands exactly on a mark. Find the mark labeled 40.

validator.description: Student set Blue Team bar to any other wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Axis mark at 40 highlighted.

beats.text: Blue Team scored 40. That ends in 0, so it lands right on a mark. Look at the axis: 0, 10, 20, 30, 40. Place the bar at 40.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates: highlights axis mark at 40, then draws bar directly to that mark. Bar locks at 40.

beats.text: Watch. Blue Team scored 40. The number ends in 0, so with Scale of 10, it lands exactly on a tick mark. I find 40 on the axis and place the bar right there. The bar for Blue Team goes to 40.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. Red Team at 25, Blue Team at 40. Green Team and Yellow Team bars empty.

beats.text: Click to place the bar for Green Team (55 points).

validator.description: Student set Green Team bar to 55

params.description: Green Team bar locks at 55.

beats.text: Right. 55, halfway between 50 and 60.

validator.description: Student set Green Team bar to 50 on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Green Team scored 55, not 50. Where does 55 land?

validator.description: Student set Green Team bar to 60 on first attempt

beats.text: Check the data table. How many points did Green Team score?

validator.description: Student set Green Team bar to any other wrong height on first attempt

beats.text: Look at the data table. Green Team scored 55. That's halfway between two marks.

validator.description: Student set Green Team bar to 50 on second attempt

params.description: Green Team row highlighted showing value 55.

beats.text: Green Team scored 55, not 50. Look at the axis: find 50 and 60. Where does 55 go between them?

validator.description: Student set Green Team bar to 60 on second attempt

params.description: Axis marks at 50 and 60 highlighted.

beats.text: Green Team scored 55, not 60. Look at the marks for 50 and 60. 55 is exactly halfway between those two marks.

validator.description: Student set Green Team bar to any other wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Axis marks at 50 and 60 highlighted with midpoint indicator.

beats.text: Green Team scored 55. Find 50 and 60 on the axis. 55 is exactly halfway between them. Place the bar at that midpoint.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates: highlights axis marks at 50 and 60, shows midpoint indicator at 55, then draws bar to that height. Bar locks at 55.

beats.text: Watch. Green Team scored 55. I find 50 and 60 on the axis. 55 is halfway between them. With Scale of 10, numbers ending in 5 land at the midpoint. The bar for Green Team goes to 55.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. Red Team at 25, Blue Team at 40, Green Team at 55. Yellow Team bar empty.

beats.text: Click to place the bar for Yellow Team (70 points).

validator.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to 70

params.description: Yellow Team bar locks at 70.

beats.text: Right. On 70.

validator.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to 60 on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Yellow Team scored 70, not 60. Find 70 on the axis.

validator.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to 80 on first attempt

beats.text: Check the data table. How many points did Yellow Team score?

validator.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to any other wrong height on first attempt

beats.text: Look at the data table. Yellow Team scored 70. That's right on a mark.

validator.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to 60 on second attempt

params.description: Yellow Team row highlighted showing value 70.

beats.text: Yellow Team scored 70, not 60. The number ends in 0, so it lands exactly on a mark. Find the mark labeled 70.

validator.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to 80 on second attempt

params.description: Axis mark at 70 highlighted.

beats.text: Yellow Team scored 70, not 80. Find the mark labeled 70 on the axis. That's where the bar should end.

validator.description: Student set Yellow Team bar to any other wrong height on second attempt

params.description: Axis mark at 70 highlighted.

beats.text: Yellow Team scored 70. That ends in 0, so it lands right on a mark. Look at the axis: 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70. Place the bar at 70.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly three or more times. System models the answer.

params.description: System demonstrates: highlights axis mark at 70, then draws bar directly to that mark. Bar locks at 70.

beats.text: Watch. Yellow Team scored 70. The number ends in 0, so with Scale of 10, it lands exactly on a tick mark. I find 70 on the axis and place the bar right there. The bar for Yellow Team goes to 70.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. All bars complete. Red Team at 25, Blue Team at 40, Green Team at 55, Yellow Team at 70.

beats.text: That's it. You chose the scale and placed every bar exactly where it should be, some exactly on the lines and some halfway between.

elements.description: Data table showing Team Scores.
Complete horizontal bar graph with scale of 10. Red Team at 25, Blue Team at 40, Green Team at 55, Yellow Team at 70.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears. Favorite Colors data from previous sections.

beats.text: You learned to choose scales. Check the biggest number. Does it fit without too many marks on the axis? Check the ones place digits. Are they 0 or 5 to find the exact place for the bar? For very small data, scale of 1 could work. Then pick the easiest to read.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Favorite Colors data.

params.description: Bar graph clears from screen.

beats.text: Let's see what you know.
